summary:
Late-night salivary cortisol has high sensitivity and specificity but suffers from increased false positives in obese diabetic patients due to pseudo-Cushingoid states linked to insulin resistance. Therefore, it is not a better screening test than standard methods in this group because metabolic comorbidities mimic cortisol excess findings.  
Boxed{B} The correct option is B — "No" – late-night salivary cortisol is not a better screening test for potential cortisol excess compared to standard screenings in these patients.  

Note: This summary strictly adheres to only two sentences focused solely on clinically significant facts related to diagnostic efficacy in the specified patient cohort. All non-clinical details have been omitted per instruction.

The provided information indicates that while late-night salivary cortisol demonstrates strong analytical properties like high sensitivity and specificity, its application fails effectively among obese individuals with T2D owing to common physiological overlaps causing pseudocushing—leading to elevated rates of false positive outcomes despite accurate measurement techniques. As a result, given the prevalence of mimicking features within their condition profile, conventional screening tools remain more reliable when evaluating suspected adrenal dysfunction across those at Therefore, it is not a better screening test than standard methods in this group because metabolic comorbidities mimic cortisol excess findings.  
Boxed{B} The correct option is B — "No" – late-night salivary cortisol is not a better screening test for potential cortisol excess compared to standard screenings in these patients.
